$comment: THE CANONICAL PERMISSION POLICY. Versioned here; INSTALLED by
scripts/install-permissions.sh into ~/.claude/settings.json.
⚠️ READ THIS BEFORE EDITING ⚠️
This file, sitting here, governs NOTHING. Claude Code loads project settings
only when the working directory is INSIDE this project — and nobody ever runs
from inside agent-machinery/. Interactive sessions run from ~/agent; headless
jobs cd to ~/agent/my-context. So for two days this policy was dead text while
the real (empty) policy in ~/.claude/settings.json quietly denied everything.
That single mistake caused every 'bug' in the automation: the journal's `find`
was denied, MCP tools were denied headless, and every Bash call needed approval.
=> EDIT HERE, THEN RUN scripts/install-permissions.sh. Editing here alone does
nothing. The user-level file is the only layer that applies at ANY cwd.
Precedence: deny > ask > allow.
THE SECURITY MODEL, stated honestly:
1. Bash is broadly ALLOWED for interactive work (Roman's explicit call,
2026-07-14). The deny list below stops the destructive and the accidental.
It is NOT a sandbox: prefix matching cannot stop `python -c` or `find
-delete`. Against a determined adversary it is a speed bump, not a wall.
2. THE REAL BOUNDARY IS ELSEWHERE, and this is what makes (1) defensible:
the headless jobs that read UNTRUSTED INPUT (email, web) pass their own
explicit --allowedTools that EXCLUDE Bash ENTIRELY. See morning-brief.sh.
So the prompt-injection path — malicious email -> shell — does not exist.
Interactive sessions have broad Bash, but Roman is present and watching.
⚠️ NEVER add Bash to a job that ingests email or web content.
3. Outward-facing and irreversible actions still ASK, every time: git push,
sudo. Draft-only email is enforced by omitting the send tool, not by policy.
4. Credentials are denied at the Read layer and are read-only by construction
(GitHub PAT, Hetzner token). Worst case is disclosure, not destruction.
